Notes: When glucose production is insufficient, the body shifts to a lipid-based economy. However, the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids for energy, so the production of ketones is protective (1, 2). In summary, the slow rate of b-oxidation of fatty acids seems to be a unique intrinsic feature of the brain tissue, particularly of the mitochondria of neurons. For comparison, in the heart and kidney tissue, 60% to 80% of the energy need is provided by fatty acid oxidation. Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

When glucose production is insufficient, the body shifts to a lipid-based economy. However, the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids for energy, so the production of ketones is protective (1, 2). In summary, the slow rate of b-oxidation of fatty acids seems to be a unique intrinsic feature of the brain tissue, particularly of the mitochondria of neurons. For comparison, in the heart and kidney tissue, 60% to 80% of the energy need is provided by fatty acid oxidation. Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

When glucose production is insufficient, the body shifts to a lipid-based economy. However, the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids for energy, so the production of ketones is protective (1, 2). In summary, the slow rate of b-oxidation of fatty acids seems to be a unique intrinsic feature of the brain tissue, particularly of the mitochondria of neurons. For comparison, in the heart and kidney tissue, 60% to 80% of the energy need is provided by fatty acid oxidation. Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

When glucose production is insufficient, the body shifts to a lipid-based economy. However, the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids for energy, so the production of ketones is protective (1, 2). In summary, the slow rate of b-oxidation of fatty acids seems to be a unique intrinsic feature of the brain tissue, particularly of the mitochondria of neurons. For comparison, in the heart and kidney tissue, 60% to 80% of the energy need is provided by fatty acid oxidation. Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

When glucose production is insufficient, the body shifts to a lipid-based economy. However, the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids for energy, so the production of ketones is protective (1, 2). In summary, the slow rate of b-oxidation of fatty acids seems to be a unique intrinsic feature of the brain tissue, particularly of the mitochondria of neurons. For comparison, in the heart and kidney tissue, 60% to 80% of the energy need is provided by fatty acid oxidation. Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

When glucose production is insufficient, the body shifts to a lipid-based economy. However, the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids for energy, so the production of ketones is protective (1, 2). In summary, the slow rate of b-oxidation of fatty acids seems to be a unique intrinsic feature of the brain tissue, particularly of the mitochondria of neurons. For comparison, in the heart and kidney tissue, 60% to 80% of the energy need is provided by fatty acid oxidation. Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

When glucose production is insufficient, the body shifts to a lipid-based economy. However, the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids for energy, so the production of ketones is protective (1, 2). In summary, the slow rate of b-oxidation of fatty acids seems to be a unique intrinsic feature of the brain tissue, particularly of the mitochondria of neurons. For comparison, in the heart and kidney tissue, 60% to 80% of the energy need is provided by fatty acid oxidation. Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. There is relatively low capacity of antioxidant enzymes in brain as compared with other organs. mass). We argue that, as a consequence, the overall process starting from the liberation of NEFA in the white adipose tissue to their oxidation in the matrix compartment of neural mitochondria is much too slow for supplying enough ATP to neurons with rapid sustained firing.

When glucose production is insufficient, the body shifts to a lipid-based economy. However, the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids for energy, so the production of ketones is protective (1, 2). In summary, the slow rate of b-oxidation of fatty acids seems to be a unique intrinsic feature of the brain tissue, particularly of the mitochondria of neurons. For comparison, in the heart and kidney tissue, 60% to 80% of the energy need is provided by fatty acid oxidation. Low-level oxidation of long-chain fatty acids by isolated brain mitochondria has been attributed to (i) the low translocation rate of long-chain fatty acid-CoA esters across inner mitochondrial membrane and (ii) the low enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation pathway. The first suggestion is supported by the discovery that the brain-specific isoenzyme of the carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 has a low activity. Moreover, the enzymatic capacity of the b-oxidation in brain mitochondria is remarkably lower than in mitochondria from the other highenergy turnover tissues. Particularly, the 3 ketoacyl-coenzyme A thiolase activity, the terminal enzyme of the four steps of the b-oxidation pathway, is very low. The activity of this enzyme in the brain has only 0.7% of that in the rat heart mitochondria. The other enzymes of the b-oxidation pathway, such as the acyl-CoA dehydrogenase or the enoyl-CoA-dehydrogenase have ~50% and 19% of that of the heart mitochondria. Therefore, it seems likely that evolution has developed a strategy to minimize ROS generation in neuronal cells, which moreover have a very poor regenerative potential. According to a recent hypothesis, the ROS prevention strategy consists in the abolishment of the b-oxidation in neurons. Although tryptophan oxygenase is absent from the kidney, this organ does contain high levels of kynureninase, kynurenine 3-hydroxylase, and kynurenine aminotransferase (De Castro, Brown, and Price, 1957) which may metabolize further the kynurenine produced in the liver.

Liver mitochondria generate large amounts of ketone bodies but do not consume them, and the suppression of SCOT (succinyl-CoA:3-ketoacid CoA transferase) expression in liver can be viewed as a mechanism to avoid futile cycling.

The activity of phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase, one of the rate-limiting enzymes in gluconeogenesis, is very low in lung tissue (38), suggesting that the gluconeogenic pathway is not of major importance. Therefore, circulating glucose is the probable major source of lung intracellular carbohydrate. In gluconeogenic tissues such as the liver and perhaps the fetal lung, glucose 6-phosphate can be hydrolyzed to glucose or converted to yield glycogen. In glycolytic tissues such as the adult lung, however, it either enters the Embden-Meyerhof pathway of glycolysis or is oxidized to phosphogluconate and ribulose 5-phosphate by the pentose cycle.
References: PMID: 6376097, Donald F. Tierney, Stephen L. Young. Glucose and Intermediary Metabolism of the Lungs. Compr Physiol 2011, Supplement 10: Handbook of Physiology, The Respiratory System, Circulation and Nonrespiratory Functions: 255-275. First published in print 1985. doi: 10.1002/cphy.cp030106.
